text: The surgeon makes three small incisions around the umbilicus and inserts the surgical ports into these incisions.
The surgeon places purse-string sutures around the ports, followed by bringing the robotic system into the operative field and securing it to the ports.
Three ports are visibly inserted through a single incision, and three additional ports are inserted through a Single Incision Laparoscopic Surgery (SILS) port.

The surgeon isolates the ileocolic pedicle as the first step in the single incision right colon procedure. The omentum is moved to the upper abdomen, and a hook cautery is used to dissect the ileocolic vessels.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum as it travels underneath the dissected ileocolic vessels, visible within the surgical field.

The surgeon introduces a ligature through one of the robotic ports or a laparoscopic trocar. The robotic arm remains docked to the port, with the instrument removed, allowing the robotic arm to be used alongside the handheld ligature.
Following the transection of the ileocolic pedicle, the surgeon begins lateral mobilization of the colon, using instruments to carefully separate the colon from the lateral attachments.
In the robotic version of the procedure, the surgeon uses a hook electrocautery device, while in the standard laparoscopy, scissors with cautery are employed for dissection.
The surgeon focuses on completing the lateral mobilization of the colon, ensuring the surrounding tissues are adequately freed and repositioned.
In the laparoscopic procedure, the surgeon uses a ligature to mobilize the hepatic flexure. In the robotic procedure, the duodenum is being carefully mobilized away from the colonic mesentery.
In the robotic version of the colectomy, the surgeon has completed the takedown of the lateral attachments, leaving the colon free from its lateral connections.
The surgeon extracts the specimens from the abdominal cavity and performs an extracorporeal anastomosis, connecting the bowel segments outside the body.
